exerciseAudio.text: Before proceeding, check the calibration before thirty-one minutes have passed.

scenes.narration: At an international summer school, I struggled to follow lectures and conversations in English. As a researcher, I had little dedicated study time. Meanwhile, AI tools repeatedly made me wait.

AIterval connects them. It observes only semantic generation-state controls—never my prompt or the response. After the threshold, one pre-authored listening exercise appears. I play the sentence, choose an answer, get clear feedback, and turn lost seconds into recovered learning time.

Here is the released Manifest V3 extension in a deterministic fixture. The host AI becomes ready while speech is still playing. The readable notice appears, but audio continues naturally and every exercise control remains available. I can finish the question or use Return to AI now.

Those tiny sessions form a learning system: recovered time, completed exercises, a review queue, weak skills, difficulty and topic preferences, and English voice selection. All 132 original, pre-authored exercises work locally without an API key.

Privacy is a product constraint. AIterval never reads prompts or responses. It observes only narrow generation-state controls. Learning progress remains local, with no analytics, advertising, tracking SDK, or remote extension code.

I supplied the lived problem, core interaction, product direction, privacy boundaries, and final design decisions. Codex with GPT-5.6 implemented and refined the state machine, browser adapters, interface, accessibility, tests, deployment, and release workflow. The public repository, architecture notes, tests, and green continuous integration make that inspectable. GPT-5.6 supported development; it does not generate exercises at runtime.
AIterval does not ask busy people to find more time. It turns time they already lose into a learning habit.
